speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two this time. It's March 24, 1996. This is Bruce Einhorn interviewing Professor Al Steer in Washington, DC, in English. Now, Professor, you said that you were pulling strings to get transferred to sea duty. And you had some trouble, and you were talking about an Admiral Wilkinson.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Admiral Wilkinson was the admiral to whom I report every morning. And when I first put in my request for sea duty, I had mentioned this to Admiral Wilkinson in one of these morning get-togethers. And he, being a seagoing man, approved. He says, fine, I think it's a good idea. He says, I'll approve. And so when my application got over to BUPERS, where my friends, you see, where the underlings were, there was a long-hand, written endorsement on the bottom of my request-- a favorable endorsement signed by Admiral Wilkinson.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so what happened-- another superior of mine realized that if I left, he'd have to find somebody else that spoke German. And that was too much trouble. So he took a pair of scissors, and he snipped off the admiral's [LAUGHS] endorsement. But he didn't snip too good, and a part of the upper line was left on the original.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so in my file today, in the Navy Department, is this short, skirted letter with a few-- anyway, so I say, what the hell am I going to do? And then my friends, you know, were in the-- in the BUPERS. And well, finally they said they didn't get enough people. They're going to put it out again. And I said, great. And I said, how about putting it through this time? And so they did. And the second time around, I made it in. And-- And where were you posted?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- [LAUGHS] you ever hear of the 90-Day Wonders?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you tell us about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. They would take-- the Navy would take recent graduates from college and send them to one of two places, either Northwestern University or that old battleship in New York called the Prairie State, and give them a 90-day quick brush-up on navigation, and seamanship, and so forth, and boom-- send them out to sea. But for us who had already been commissioned into the Navy-- by this time, I had a whole year, you see, in the Navy. I was an old veteran. [LAUGHS] They had a special shortened version of that, of 60 days. So I wasn't a 90-Day Wonder. I was a 60-Day Wonder. [LAUGHS] Well, I learned a lot that was very useful, and I was very grateful for it. I mean, I learned night seamanship, you know, everything about navigation, and all that sort of thing. I learned-- And so then they sent me out. And they sent me to Boston-- Boston Harbor, as a fifth wheel on a mine-sweep, just to get a little sea doing. And I learned a lot about that. Then they sent me as Prospective Commanding Officer to an APc, which was essentially a Boston fishing dragger hull-- wood-- diesel propelled, three officers and 21 crew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were supposed to carry small detachments of Navy-- Army in inner island [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] invasions. I could carry 80 men. And this is what I was supposed to be doing, you see? Were you still an ensign?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still an ensign, en-sigh-gan, yes. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you posted during this? What was your base of operation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first-- my first-- I went to the-- the ship was being built in Warren, Rhode Island. And boy, if you want a perfect little mini-Massachusetts, New England town, that is still just gorgeous. I had a great time. I was there for two months waiting for it to be finished, and so forth, and so on. And then we went up the coast. And I-- I-- I did some training with the entire ship with COMDESLANT, which means Commander, Destroyers, Atlantic, out of Portland, Maine. Middle of winter-- cold! Oh brother, it was cold. And then they set us down [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] starting on us on our way to the Southwest Pacific. But we got caught in this hell of a storm off of Hatteras. And-- and we damn near sank. As a matter of fact, three small ships did sink-- Navy ships did sink in that storm. And they pulled us in at Charleston Navy Yard. And they said, my god, you're top heavy. And I said, yeah, I think so. And they said, well, we put some special ballast in you. Anyway-- and then we went on down to-- to Miami. We pulled into Miami.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I met, in Miami, a guy who I had known in the Boston mine-sweep. He says, why don't you come here? He says, this is the sub-chaser training center. We've got much better ships here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, well, hell, I got orders to this ship. I'm commanding officer of the ship. Well, he says, we can fix it. We can fix it. He says, we've got pull with the Navy department. Well, they had a PC there. And I took one look at the PC-- I fell in love with the first PC I ever saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us what a PC is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stands for patrol craft, and it looks like a junior destroyer. It's 180 feet, steel, twin diesel propulsion, 1800 horses in each shaft. It'll go like a bat out of hell. And [LAUGHS] I was in love that ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, you suppose I could get a PC? He says, yeah, you can get a PC. Well, OK. So I-- I put in for it. And they called Washington on the phone, and I got-- and I got telephone orders the next day. And I'm telling you, I mean, you see, you have to manage your own career in the Navy, at least in those days. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And so they put me through this special training course at Miami, and then sent me out to a PC, which was-- I picked one up in Portland, Oregon. And we went up and went to the Aleutians. Well, I wasn't-- I wasn't skipper at that point. I was exec, the second in command. And then we had a very hairy six weeks-- six months in the Aleutians, and went down to Pearl Harbor, under which I took command. And we steamed around to the Central Pacific for a while. And then I was in the invasion of Saipan, and then so forth, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the Pacific Theatre?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the 4th of July, 1900 and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] gosh, that must have been '43. Or was it '42? It must have been '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: Until when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when the war ended in the Pacific?

speaker: interviewee
text: By this time, I had gone up another ship. I was commanding officer of-- of an APD, which was the old Navy destroyer from World War I, the four-pipers, one of the old four-pipers. You ever hear of them? Maybe you have. They were-- the Navy built 345 of them at the end of World War I. They had them tied up in various estuaries, you know, 15 or 20 abreast, swinging there for years between the two wars. I had one of those. We got hit by a kamikaze off of Okinawa, and I came home. And I was in-- I was in-- I was in San Diego waiting for the Navy to decide what to do next with this damaged ship when they dropped the A-bomb.

speaker: interviewer
text: And between the time you began your service in the Pacific and the time you were in San Diego, did you see actual combat at sea?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. They sent me out to Saipan, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was to be a control vessel. Now, when you send-- you see these pictures of all these landing craft full of marines heading towards the beach. Well, they have to head toward a specific beach, and they have to be guided in, because those guys are ducking bullets. And they have to be backed up, and so forth. And so they would take this PC of mine-- I was the closest in of any of the ships-- 1,000 yards off the beach. And I had to be-- I was off of Green Beach One and Green Beach Two. I was the commanding-- I was the control vessel for those two beaches.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Saipan area?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Saipan, right. And the boys would come in with these landing craft, and they would line up on me before D-day, before H-hour. And I would line them up this side, line them up this side. And then when the thing got out, I'd-- boom-- blow the whistle, and they'd take off, you see. And then I was the-- I was the radio link between the guys ashore and all of the supplies that were in these floating freighters astern, four or five miles out at sea where they were safe from artillery fire from the islands, you see. And first, they come through-- well, we need more barbed wire. Well, I knew that barbed wire is in the second hold in AKA such and such. And I'd send a boat out, and they'd send barbed wire. Or it was k-rations, or it was-- who knows what, you see. And so-- and then the Japs would keep trying to shoot me. This involved a lot of radio traffic, you know, back and forth. And the Japs were into that, and they had identified me as the key element in a lot of this radio traffic. [LAUGHS] And I was standing up on the flying bridge with this dumb Army colonel. And we could see-- this is when the invasion had gone several hours. This was late in day, or middle of the day or later on, on D-day. And here comes a shell hit the water just ahead of us, about 100 yards ahead of us, you see, from the Japs. And I said, Jesus, they're shooting at us. And the colonel says, you know they are. So here comes another one about 50 yards ahead of us. [LAUGHS] And the colonel says, Captain, you better get the hell out of here. I said, well, I'm supposed to do a job. I don't know. And so I'm standing there. The next shell come up exactly where the last one had hit. And then the colonel says, that's their extreme range. I guess you guys are safe. Hoo-- that was the-- and D plus one, we were here, and Tinian-- the island of Tinian was over there, only about three miles away.

speaker: interviewer
text: D plus one. Tell the-- tell us where D plus one is.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. D is D-day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- for us, it was the 15th of June, 1940-- well, what was-- what was D-day in Normandy? This was one week later.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK. And this was in, what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was it '43 or '44? Four-- '44. Anyway, so this was D plus one. I'm sitting there. And I came on-- I was supposed to anchor the ship within 50 yards of the designated geographic position. Now, that's not particularly hard if you've got good visibility and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got up there, and I came in, and I took a visual fix. I don't know if you know about taking a fix. I took a fix. And I had the two tangents on the island-- boom, boom, here, and here. And I cut it down the middle with a cupola on a-- on a little administrative building in the-- in the town of Garapan right over there. This is at Saipan?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Saipan, yes. And so the next morning, I went on the deck to see daylight to check my fix to see I hadn't moved overnight. It was anchored, you see? And I got it-- but, goddammit, I couldn't find that town of Garapan-- gone. What had happened was-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] the Army had-- you've got visitors. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, you were saying that you were near Saipan, and you couldn't find that town, because it had disappeared.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't find the town. It disappeared. What had happened was they had dropped leaflets over the entire island saying that every civilian target would be spared if they didn't use it for military purposes. So in the middle of night, the Marines call out-- I mean, radioed out-- saying the Japs are forming up a counterattack in the town of Garapan. Well, the entire US Navy opened up on Garapan. And friend, come daylight, there wasn't any Garapan left at all. I later walked through it, and there was nothing but rubble-- heaps of rubble, a wasteland, nothing. Everything-- so anyway, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] this some-- well, they started shooting at me from sight from Tinian, which was down about three miles to my right. And all of a sudden, here comes a shell, and it was still headed at me-- they were still after me-- but it landed about 75 yards short. In other words, at broadside. We were like this, and over there-- about 75 yards. Then I thought, holy cow, that's probably an accident. Because the Navy was supposed to have silenced all the shore bomb-- shore artillery on Tinian, but apparently, this one they had overlooked or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: The next thing I know, there's a explosion on the opposite side of me, about 100 yards out. You know what that means? If you know anything about artillery, this is a bracket, and I'm right in the middle. So all the cannoneer has got to do is split the difference, and whack-o, I'm going to catch it the next time, see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fortunately, there was about a three-minute interval between the two shots. And they were having difficulty, apparently, getting the shots out. Apparently bombardment had damaged the gun, but hadn't completely knocked it out. So I told the boys, drop the-- I mean, slip the hook, which means let the chain go, let it go overboard. But put a buoy on it, you see. And I started and backed out. And we were also headquarters for a whole bunch of these stranded boat crews whose ships had gone away and left them. They would come and tie up astern of us, and come on board, and get something to eat, and a cup of coffee. And I'm backing up, and all these boat crews trying to get out of the way. And as I backed away, I could see the swirl in the water where my propellers had started to grab the water. And, boom, as I backed away, the third shot hit right in that swirl of water. I-- [LAUGHS] so of course, we reported by radio, and about four destroyers converged on Tinian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in about an hour, I got the word, it's safe now. You can go back. But if you ever try to pick up-- you've probably never tried to pick up a slipped anchor-- woo-hoo. [LAUGHS] Well, we did it. But it was a pain the ass. And so-- now where the hell was I? Well, you were in San Diego. And I'm wondering, when you're in San Diego and the Japanese surrender, what happens next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- yeah, they decided-- now this is on-- I'm on the roper now, this old four-piper that had been damaged by the kamikaze. No? OK. And the question was, were they going to spend time and the Navy's money fixing up this old four-piper that was 20-odd years old at this point, or just scrap it and send a new ship, see? And they sent a-- a Navy Board of Inspection and Survey on board to decide this. And it was headed by an admiral, and the second in command was a Captain, and they had a third, a commander, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a-- I was a Lieutenant in all my glory-- two big stripes, you see. And-- [LAUGHS] and-- and so anyway, they all-- I thought I had a good-- I know I had a good ship, a very good ship. And I was walking-- after all-- everybody who was supposed to make these inspections, they would come back and report to the admiral. And I was walking with admiral, pacing back and forth on my deck, waiting for these reports to come. And finally, [LAUGHS] the guy-- the Captain, Navy Captain, [? now force ?] driver, who had inspected the engineering spaces, he changed into dungarees in my quarters. And he crawled through-- they told me that he crawled through the bilges on his hands and knees tapping every rivet head. Brother, did we get an inspection. And-- [LAUGHS] and so he came up onto the main deck to report to the Captain, the comm-- the admiral. And he put his arm or hand on my stack, I remember. And by the way, my chief-- engineering room chief was behind him. And he says, admiral, I have been engineering officer on these ships. I've been commanding officer on these ships. I have been division officer on these ships. And that's the best goddamn engine room I ever saw. Well, my chief was just going like this. I mean, proud as he could be. So-- so anyway, they decided to fix us up. But the chief couldn't take prosperity. That night, he went ashore, and he hung one on-- you couldn't believe. They-- shore patrol brought him back with a list, you know, this long. He'd taken over some striptease joint, and the stripteaser hadn't taken off enough. And he went up on the stage to help her take it off. And [LAUGHS] he got in a fight with a shore patrol. And he hit the-- blacked the shore patrol officer's eye. And I am telling you, I had a court martial on my-- I had a court martial. That was another story. But anyway, that's what I did in San Diego.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they-- they sent us. They said, we're going to repair you, but it'll be a low priority. And so there were a lot of ships that had damage to their engine room spaces. We didn't. Our damage was all forward. And therefore, they said, we will send you up to San Francisco and tie you up to a ship and you can supply steam for yourself and for this other ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get to San Francisco?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we got to San Francisco on [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about the end of-- the last half of August of '45, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long we were you in San Francisco?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, and then we stayed there until VE Day-- or VJ day, it is. And then the Navy came through, oh, there's no sense of keeping his old hooker in commission any longer. And I took up her to Mare Island, and we decommissioned her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is Mare Island?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mare Island is at the north end of the San Francisco Bay Area, and it's a great Navy installation up there. Has been for a long time. And from there, I went back to Washington, and then Nuremberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you went back to Washington, when your ship was decommissioned, where were you posted?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't posted. I still-- I had a lot of leave time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was taking my leave time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us how you came into contact with Nuremberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- well, on this ship, I had a good friend, [? Rouf ?] [? Strau, ?] and we decided-- we'd seen this little ship that the Army had called an FS. And we had decided that it would be a good idea to have two or three FS's and work out among the Pacific Islands, taking small amounts of cargo-- principally Copra-- and collect them, and bring them into centers like Singapore, and Manila, and so forth and so on, for transhipment on larger, full-size freighters, you see. But with this little FS, you see, you'd get into the lagoons-- all right. So I was to go to Washington and get some of these FS's from surplus. And [? Rouf ?] was going to raise some money from a friend of his who was a banker in San Francisco.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was a commercial venture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we-- we were both getting out of the Navy. We knew we were going to get out of the Navy. And, well, I got down to-- finally got to the place where these FSes were supposed to be held-- managed. And I found out that I had been beaten by one day by, guess whom? Moran Shipping and Tugboats from New York City. They had them all, and were reselling them at about four or five times the price and I-- we couldn't afford them. I was feeling really down. And I called up [? Rouf ?] in San Francisco, and I said, [? Rouf, ?] I said, I'm sorry, but this isn't going to work. We can't get the ships. And he says, well, I got news for you. I can't get the money. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were feeling real down in the mouth. I was. And I went around to the Mayflower Hotel on Connecticut Avenue and I was having a few drinks and feeling sorry for myself, and everything. And I happened to be talking to some Army major. And we were weeping on each other's shoulders. And I mentioned somehow that I could speak German. He says, why don't you go to Nuremberg? I said, what the hell is at Nuremberg? Oh, don't you know? They're going to try these war criminals. Huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: First I'd heard of it. You know, I'd been out in the Pacific. And he says, yeah, there's some colonel over in the-- in the Pentagon that's hiring people. His name is Dostert. And, well, I was supposed to be at the Pentagon the next day anyway. So I went over and I saw Dostert. Two days later, I left for the Atlantic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who was--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, planned-- [LAUGHS] see, I mean, you have to-- in the Navy, in those days, you had to manage your own career.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now do you remember the month and year when you went to see this Dostert?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, yes. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately.

speaker: interviewee
text: September, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who was Dostert?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dostert was a colonel. First of all, he was born in France and spent much of his youth and education in the United States. He spoke both French and English perfectly, American English.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was he doing in the Pentagon?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, he was-- and he had been in the Army, and he had made interpreting a-- a career. And he was Eisenhower's interpreter during Northern France-- and first of all, Northern Italy, and then France, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the US Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: US, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to see him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so he-- he said-- I said, I know something about Germany. I don't like Hitler's [INAUDIBLE] and I would like to be in on the final scene. Well, he says, first of all, we've got to test you. I said, what do you mean, test me? He said, to see if you really know German.

speaker: interviewer
text: He didn't know enough German to tell whether I knew any German or not, you see? So I'm going to send you over to the-- to the State Department. Well, all right, I'll take a test. So I went over to the State Department. [LAUGHS] They gave me-- the guy that gave me a test didn't know as much German as I did. [LAUGHS] Anyway, the next day, I-- two days later, I flew the Atlantic. And-- Two days later, you were already on the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yup. Yup.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk to folks about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: By that time, I was in charge. Yes, of course, I talked to them. But they didn't raise any objections.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you single?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, still. I hadn't had time to collect any females, friend. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay in the Navy to go to Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The Navy, at that point, had a system of getting people out. They had a certain number of points. And the married men automatically got more points than a single man, which is perfectly honest. I had no objections. But as a single man, I had to serve, I think, four months longer before I would be eligible for release. And I thought-- and he-- and Dostert told me, and he says, if you go to Nuremberg, you're not going to get out after any four months. He says, you're going to have to sign up for as long in the Navy as it takes to finish the job at Nuremberg. I said, all right, I'll do it. But I want so badly to see the end of this goddamned thing that I'll sacrifice whatever-- two, three, five months, whatever it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still a Lieutenant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, god no. I was Lieutenant Commander. Didn't I tell you about that? Oh. At every point in the Navy, for the reserves and those [INAUDIBLE], after about a year and a half of satisfactory, they push you up a grade, see? And I got to be a Lieutenant Junior grade. Then I got to be Lieutenant. That's a-- it came with the Captain.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of a sudden, promotion stopped. And then I would-- people would come to me and say, listen-- the next-- by the next promotion would be to Lieutenant Commander, and that would be the first time that the Navy's famous selection board would cut in and choose me, specifically, by-- on my record for promotion. And-- Navy-wide, not depending on some commanding officer that you'd boot-licked, you see? That's the way the Army did it, but not the Navy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I was very anxious to have the Navy's selection board look at my record. I thought it was pretty good. And I would get a promotion that I would figure I had earned. No. No. Finally, they called my group. For two years and nine months, we got no promotion. And they finally called us Super Lieutenants, you see. [LAUGHS] Lieutenant, JG Lieutenant, and Super. All right. So then I got-- just after I got off the Roper and when I went to-- home on leave, I got this note, I was promoted to Lieutenant Commander.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was that, just so we know?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- would have been in September. Late August or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, September. September, '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So when you went to Nuremberg, you were a Lieutenant Commander.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lieutenant Commander, right. So anyway, then I went to Washington-- went back to Washington. And while I was there talking to Dostert, I went over to the Navy's Arlington annex where they had this BUPERS, and all my old-- I was looking up my old friends. Well, most of them had gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: But anyway, I went and I said, what the hell happened? And they said, don't you know? I said, no. They said, you came up, and your group came up for-- for selection to Lieutenant Commander. And let's say-- I don't have the figures in my head. But let's say there were 2,500 of you of which 2,000 were to be promoted.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the selection board had then to choose the 2,000 to go up. And they would make their decision, and they would send it up to the head of the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] promotion division within the-- within BUPERS, who was a regular Navy man who wasn't at sea, because he had fouled up badly and had been condemned to shore duty forever.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, one of those guys? Bitter, and angry, and nasty. And he took one look. He says, 2,000 reserve Lieutenant Commanders. That's too goddamn many reserves. Throw the whole thing out and have them  do it again-- three times. This is why I was-- all right. [LAUGHS] [? Forestall, ?] who had been Sec Nav, you know, Secretary of Navy, heard about it, and he promoted us all. Now, how did you get from the States to Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Flew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you arrive in Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: In November of '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you ever been in Nuremberg itself before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, several times. Several times. When I-- that year I was in-- in Germany in '35 and '36, I had visited Nuremberg I think twice. It was a great tourist attraction. It still is.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it look like when you arrived in September of 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. I-- I flew in on a DC-3 at about 10,000 feet. You could see the ground very clearly. And as we flew in from Paris-- I-- I had crossed the Atlantic and landed in Paris. Then we took a-- a flight over to Nuremberg. And I wasn't looking down at Nuremberg. I was looking down into the houses at Nuremberg. Every roof was gone. Nothing left but-- but walls, vertical walls. And not all of them, by any means. It was the most awesome destruction I have ever seen. The only thing worse I ever saw was that little town of Garapan on Saipan. Because many of these walls were two stories high, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: This would be a good time to take a break, change tapes. Thank you.
